text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic cholecystectomy by addressing omental adhesions attached to the liver edge and anterior abdominal wall, carefully dissecting these adhesions to expose the gallbladder.
The surgeon continues to expose the gallbladder, removing omental adhesions from its surface to prepare for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon successfully retracts the omentum, revealing the colon beneath. A grasper is inserted to elevate the gallbladder, and attachments between the colon and gallbladder are carefully dissected. The gallbladder is slightly elevated into the liver bed, and the surgeon checks the colon's condition, confirming it appears unharmed.

The surgeon removes the last epiploic appendage to reveal the base of the gallbladder. The gallbladder is adhered to the liver, requiring further dissection to allow for higher retraction. The surgeon alternates between dissecting the top and bottom areas to free the gallbladder.

The surgeon decompresses the gallbladder, revealing the outline of large stones within. The improved visibility allows for a clearer view of the gallbladder's base, facilitating proper dissection. The dissection begins laterally and progresses medially.
The surgeon approaches medially to retract the gallbladder further off the liver bed, freeing the peritoneum to achieve a clearer view of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses ICG mode to visualize the gallbladder, revealing a large stone at the bottom. The common bile duct is visible to the right, and green fluorescence indicates the presence of the cystic duct beneath the gallbladder, which the surgeon is attempting to clear.

The surgeon navigates laterally around the gallbladder, encountering bleeding during dissection. Blunt dissection is used to manage the bleeding, while the surgeon identifies the artery to the right of the hook and clears fat to visualize the cystic duct and Calot's triangle.
The surgeon identifies a posterior artery and uses a Maryland dissector to coagulate it. The surgeon attempts a top-down approach, visibly manipulating the artery.

The surgeon encounters bleeding while dissecting the gallbladder, creating a small hole in the gallbladder wall. A sponge is used for tamponade to achieve hemostasis, pushing up on the liver to continue dissection. The Maryland forceps are employed to pinch and burn areas of bleeding. Surgicil is applied to a persistent bleeding spot, followed by pressure with a sponge for two minutes. The gallbladder is used to lift and hold the sponge in place, and another sponge is brought in to maintain a clear view.

The surgeon uses an instrument as a retractor to expose the surgical field, aiming to identify the cystic duct. The ICG imaging highlights the area as a stone moves, aiding in visual confirmation. The surgeon performs posterior dissection to establish the critical view and proceeds to ligate the duct, confident it is the cystic duct, to improve gallbladder retraction.
The surgeon begins to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully searching for the artery within the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon struggles to manipulate the gallbladder due to large stones inside it. They carefully stay in an intramural plane, avoiding the liver bed to prevent further bleeding. The surgeon identifies the artery, cleans off surrounding fat, and uses ICG mode to confirm it is not a ductile structure before proceeding to take it. The pulsating artery is visible, and the surgeon works back up the gallbladder, reaching the previously made hole from the top-down approach.

The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy by placing the gallbladder, referred to as 'the enemy,' into a retrieval bag for removal. The pressure applied earlier with a sponge and Surgicil successfully controlled the liver bleeding.
